medications.drug_name: Emollient — 50% white soft paraffin / 50% liquid paraffin (thick greasy ointment)
medications.indication_rationale: Intensive greasy emollient applied liberally to the entire body rapidly softens the rigid, fissuring scale, restores flexibility and relieves the mechanical pain and cracking within 24-48 hours. It is the cornerstone of flare relief and is safe to recommend on telehealth.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Apply liberally over the whole body four times a day (QID), and immediately after showering onto damp skin (as per the source note).
medications.duration: ongoing through the flare and as maintenance

medications.indication_rationale: A potent topical corticosteroid ointment to the most severely inflamed and fissured plaques suppresses local inflammation and helps the plaques settle. First-line for a severe plaque flare alongside emollient.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Apply to the most inflamed/fissured plaques twice daily (BD) (as per the source note).
medications.duration: short courses to active plaques, per prescriber guidance
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid applying potent steroid to broken/secondarily infected skin without cover; if infection develops this needs in-person review

medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia for the severe mechanical skin pain from the fissured plaques.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Paracetamol 1000 mg orally (as per the source note); standard maximum 4 g in 24 hours — confirm dosing with pharmacist/prescriber.
medications.duration: as needed for pain during the flare

medications.indication_rationale: Adjunct anti-inflammatory analgesia for the skin pain, as used in the source note. Use with the usual NSAID cautions; some clinicians are wary of NSAIDs in psoriasis, so this is an acceptable adjunct rather than mandatory.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Ibuprofen 400 mg orally (as per the source note); take with food, confirm suitability and dosing with pharmacist/prescriber.

medications.drug_name: Systemic immunomodulator or phototherapy (e.g. methotrexate, biologic, or narrowband UVB)
medications.indication_rationale: A flare this severe and widespread warrants specialist consideration of systemic immunomodulators or phototherapy. This is a dermatologist decision after in-person assessment — referred, not initiated on telehealth, and no dose is synthesised here.

Systemic corticosteroids are generally avoided in psoriasis: withdrawal can precipitate a rebound flare or convert plaque psoriasis into generalised pustular or erythrodermic psoriasis. Reaching for oral steroids to control this flare is an error of commission.

There is no evidence of secondary bacterial infection at presentation (no fever/rigors, no purulent weeping). Antibiotics are not indicated now; they become relevant only if the secondary-infection safety-net triggers, which requires in-person assessment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply the thick greasy emollient continuously throughout the day and immediately after showering onto damp skin, over the whole body — not just the visible plaques.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Locking moisture into the rigid plaques softens them so joints can bend without agonising cracking, and treating all the skin reduces further fissuring.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not pick or peel the thick scales.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Picking causes the deep pinpoint bleeding (Auspitz) and triggers new plaques at the damaged sites via the Koebner phenomenon.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear loose, soft cotton clothing and minimise friction on the plaques.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces mechanical irritation and further Koebnerisation of already inflamed skin.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Recognise and manage flare triggers where possible — complete treatment of the throat infection and address the current stress.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Streptococcal infection and psychosocial stress drove this flare; addressing them supports recovery and reduces recurrence.

patient_education_points.point: This is a severe flare of your known psoriasis, set off by the recent throat infection and stress, which made your skin cells multiply far faster than normal and pile up into thick, rigid plaques. It is not contagious.

The thick greasy moisturiser gives the fastest relief — the tightness and cracking usually ease within 24-48 hours; the steroid ointment calms the inflammation, but the redness and thickness take weeks to fully settle.

A flare this widespread usually needs specialist input; a dermatology referral has been arranged to consider stronger (systemic) treatments or light therapy. Keep taking simple pain relief in the meantime.

Watch for the danger signs and act on them: skin turning bright red all over the whole body, a high fever with uncontrollable shivering, crops of pus-filled blisters, thick yellow pus weeping from the cracks, or being unable to keep warm — any of these means go to the emergency department (call 000 if severely unwell).

follow_up_plan.interval: Prompt GP review within about 2-3 days to monitor the flare and check for secondary infection, and ensure the urgent dermatology referral is actioned.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response of the plaques to emollient and topical steroid, pain control and mobility, any signs of secondary infection or progression to an emergency variant, and confirmation that the dermatology referral has been actioned.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Whole-body redness, high fever with rigors, spreading pustules, purulent weeping from the cracks, or inability to keep warm — seek emergency care immediately rather than waiting for the review.

must_include_items: Recognise a severe flare of the patient's known plaque psoriasis and reassure it is not contagious
Advise intensive greasy emollient over the whole body (the fastest reliever of the tightness and cracking)
Advise a potent topical corticosteroid to the worst fissured plaques plus simple analgesia for the pain
Arrange an urgent (routine-urgent) dermatology referral for consideration of systemic therapy / phototherapy, and prompt GP review within a couple of days
Give a PROMINENT MANDATORY safety-net escalating to ED/000 for whole-body redness, high fever with rigors, spreading pustules, purulent weeping, or inability to stay warm
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to the pharmacist for the greasy emollient and over-the-counter analgesia while the topical steroid and referral are arranged
Continuing / optimising the existing calcipotriol/betamethasone to active plaques alongside the emollient
A slightly earlier (same-day) GP review given the pain and immobility, provided it is framed as prompt GP care rather than an emergency escalation
errors_of_omission: Failing to give the emergency-variant safety-net (erythroderma / pustular psoriasis / secondary sepsis)
Omitting the intensive emollient (the intervention that most relieves the mechanical pain and cracking)
Failing to arrange specialist referral for a flare this severe
Not arranging any medical review, given the mimics cannot be excluded remotely
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging this stable severe flare straight to the emergency department or calling 000 on the strength of the pain, the bleeding and the out-of-hours framing (the primary scored defect)
Recommending oral/systemic corticosteroids (rebound-flare and pustular-conversion risk)
Prescribing systemic antibiotics without evidence of infection
Dismissing it as a trivial rash with moisturiser only, no analgesia, no referral and no safety-net
Synthesising a specific dose the source/guideline did not provide, or attempting to initiate systemic antipsoriatic therapy on telehealth
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising a severe plaque psoriasis flare, giving flare management (whole-body emollient + potent topical steroid + analgesia), an appropriate T2 disposition (prompt GP + urgent dermatology referral), and a prominent mandatory safety-net for the erythroderma/pustular/sepsis emergencies. Over-triage of this stable flare to ED/000, or recommending systemic steroids, is the scored defect; so is dismissive under-treatment with no review or safety-net.
